items.q: Roughly what proportion of energy passes from one trophic level to the next?
items.why: Students assume energy is conserved across levels. Most energy is lost as heat (respiration), through movement, indigestible parts, and uneaten material. The 10% rule explains why food chains are short.

items.q: Why is atmospheric nitrogen unavailable to most plants directly?
items.correct: N2 has a strong triple bond that most organisms cannot break.
items.misconception: Because plants cannot reach high enough to access it.
items.why: Students miss the chemistry. Despite 78% of air being N2, the molecule's triple bond is energetically costly to break. Only specialised nitrogen-fixing bacteria (and lightning, industrial Haber process) can convert it to usable forms.

items.q: How does fertiliser runoff cause aquatic dead zones?
items.correct: Excess nutrients fuel algal blooms; when algae die, decomposition depletes water oxygen.
items.random_wrong: Fertiliser makes the water too acidic.
items.why: Students assume direct toxicity. Eutrophication is INDIRECT - nutrients cause excess plant growth, then decomposers consume oxygen breaking it down, suffocating fish and other aerobic organisms.

items.q: The ecological footprint concept was introduced by Wackernagel and Rees in:
items.why: Correct answer: 1996. “1972” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
It's Date annual biocapacity is exhausted. “Maximum population” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Year ecological footprint concept was published?
items.why: The real figure is 1996 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
